text: The surgeon encounters flimsy omental adhesions to the gallbladder, which are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon begins dissection at the bottom of the gallbladder, observing additional structures that do not fluoresce with ICG, indicating the location of the cystic duct.

The surgeon examines a wad of tissue near the gallbladder, noting it is not enhancing with ICG, suggesting it is not the cystic duct. They work through a veil of tissue, possibly peritoneum, on the lateral aspect of the gallbladder.

The surgeon performs dissection using scissors within the hepatocystic triangle, encountering tissue on the lateral aspect that obstructs the view of the cystic duct. Medial dissection is carried out over the artery to improve visibility.

The surgeon works on the lateral aspect of the gallbladder, attempting to identify the cystic duct where it branches from the common duct. The fibrotic thickened peritoneum is dissected to reveal a clearer plane, allowing more mobility at the base of the gallbladder.

The surgeon attempts to dissect behind the gallbladder from a lateral standpoint, encountering fibrotic tissue that complicates the dissection. A sponge is used to manage bleeding, and the surgeon decides to clip the artery for better exposure within the hepatocystic triangle.

The surgeon identifies the cystic duct and begins clearing tissue from around it, encountering difficulty in accessing the area behind the duct due to dense tissue. A small amount of bleeding occurs, prompting the use of a sponge for packing. The surgeon continues working on the lateral aspect, struggling with visualization due to fused tissue on the liver side of the cystic duct, but eventually creates a window for better access.

The surgeon employs a top-down approach using robotic assistance to improve visualization and access, focusing on the tissue located on the liver side of the cystic duct. This technique allows for better exposure of the back of the gallbladder and facilitates the clearance of obstructive tissue.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to dissect through the fibrotic tissue in the liver bed, creating smoke as the gallbladder attachments are separated. The cystic duct is clearly visible, surrounded by fatty and fibrous tissue, which is being carefully dissected.

And interestingly, after freeing it up, it now fills more with the ICG retrograde flow. So that was the operation. Overall, not terrible, just very stressful for myself. Interested in your guys' thoughts on any suggestions what I could do to make that a little bit more confident of a dissection.
